title_en: Salah in Al-Muqaddimaat: Principles of Maliki Prayer Law
title_ar: الصلاة في المقدمات: أصول الصلاة المالكية
content_en:
Ibn Rushd's introductory essay on prayer in Al-Muqaddimaat establishes the foundational principles of Maliki prayer law with the systematic depth characteristic of his entire work. His approach — identifying sources of disagreement, explaining Maliki positions, and situating them within the broader scholarly tradition — makes this section one of the most intellectually stimulating treatments of Maliki prayer law available to students.

Ibn Rushd begins by classifying prayers into obligatory (fard), due (wajib, in the Maliki usage — such as the Witr prayer which the Maliki school classifies as wajib by some accounts), confirmed sunnah (sunnah mu'akkadah), and recommended (mustahabb). This classification establishes the hierarchy of prayer types before the student encounters the detailed rulings for each category.

The Maliki school's approach to the pillars (arkan) of prayer is explained with reference to the prophetic practice. Ibn Rushd explains that the Maliki school does not include the basmala (Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim) as a verse of al-Fatiha or as part of the prayer at all — it is not recited before al-Fatiha in either loud or quiet prayers. This is one of the most immediately visible practical distinctions of Maliki prayer and Ibn Rushd explains it thoroughly: Imam Malik held, based on the practice of Madinah that he witnessed personally, that al-Fatiha begins with 'Al-hamdulillah rabbil-aalamin' and that the basmala is not part of the prayer.

The question of raising the hands (raf' al-yadayn) is explained as a point on which the Maliki school differs from other schools. The Maliki position, based on the practice of Madinah in Imam Malik's era, does not include raising the hands at the beginning of ruku' or when rising from it. Raising hands is only done at the opening takbir. Ibn Rushd acknowledges that hadiths from Ibn Umar and others are used by other schools to establish a broader raf' al-yadayn practice, but explains that Imam Malik understood the practice of Madinah — as a continuous living transmission — to be more reliable evidence of the Prophet's practice than individual hadiths.

On saying Ameen, Ibn Rushd explains the Maliki position that it is said quietly in all prayers, including the loud ones. He presents this as reflecting the Maliki understanding of the communal prayer as a unified act of supplication in which the followers echo the imam's words quietly.

The Maliki school's position on the follower's recitation of al-Fatiha in congregational prayer is explained: in the loud prayers (Fajr, Maghrib, Isha), the follower does not recite al-Fatiha — the imam's recitation is sufficient. In the quiet prayers (Dhuhr, Asr), the follower recites al-Fatiha for themselves. Ibn Rushd explains this based on the Quranic command to listen when the Quran is recited (al-A'raf 7:204) and on the Maliki understanding of the hadith 'The one with an imam, the imam's recitation is sufficient for them.'

Ibn Rushd's treatment of the Friday prayer conditions reflects the Maliki school's relative leniency regarding the required congregation size and location. The Maliki school requires a minimum of twelve men (according to one position) or simply a sufficient congregation, and does not insist on the settlement being a full urban center. This flexibility reflects the practical orientation of Maliki jurisprudence and its concern for accessibility of worship for all Muslim communities.
